data.title: China lowers deposit rate ceilings to protect bank interest margins, sources say
data.description:
China has lowered the ceilings on deposit rates, three banking sources with direct knowledge of the guidance said on Friday, as authorities seek to protect banks' profit margins and discourage savings.

The interest rate self-regulatory body under China's central bank has lowered the upper limit of deposit rates banks can offer their clients, the sources said.

The move came days after China cut benchmark lending rates and state banks reduced their baseline deposit rates.

China's economy is suffering from weak consumption, a prolonged property crisis and a trade war with the United States, putting pressure on banks' profitability.

"Banks' interest margins are under heavy pressure," one of the sources said. Every 10 basis-point (bp) cut in deposit rates could reduce overall borrowing costs by roughly 5 bps, the source added.

Under the latest guidance, the ceilings for some banks' time deposit rates have been slashed by 30-40 bps, according to the sources.

In contrast, China's major banks reduced baseline deposit rates by up to 25 basis points (bps) for some tenors on Tuesday.

This reflects regulators' desire to prevent interest margins from shrinking further, as some banks compete heavily to build deposits by promising high returns.

Amid the heated competition for deposits, reducing the rate ceilings can give some breathing space for banks already suffering from sliding margins, a source said.

Commercial banks' net interest margin - a key profitability measure - dropped to a record low 1.43% in the first quarter of this year, official data showed.

The margins are expected to fall a further 10-15 bps this year, analysts at China International Capital Corp predicted.

The PBOC did not respond to Reuters' request for comment.

data.title: Higher Term Premium Needed to Stabilize U.S. Treasurys — Market Talk
data.description:
0715 GMT - The market likely needs more term premium--additional yield incentive to hold long-dated bonds rather than a short-dated bonds--for U.S. Treasurys to stabilize, Citi says in a global asset allocation view. "Even if the U.S. budget should not be as fiscally expensive as the more bearish observers suggest, the curve may stay steep." Given that U.S. year-on-year inflation is likely to rise, Citi sticks to an underweight position in U.S. Treasurys. However, the timing of a U.S. rates selloff is hard to predict, it says. The yield curve steepening--where the gap between short- and long-dated bond yields widens--has also been a global phenomenon, not just driven by U.S. Treasurys. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0657 GMT - Singapore's core inflation is likely to be modest for the rest of this year, DBS senior economist Chua Han Teng writes in a note. This reflects DBS's expectations for contained imported inflation and lower pass-through of business costs to consumer prices. Core CPI in April rose 0.7% from a year earlier, up from March's 0.5% increase. Echoing MAS's still-dovish assessment, Chua expects downside risks to inflation. A slowdown in global trade could hurt Singapore's export-reliant economy, weakening domestic labor demand and wage growth, he says.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0651 GMT - The dollar edges lower as worries over mounting U.S. government debt continue to weigh after the House passed Trump's tax and spending bill Thursday. The bill aims to make the tax cuts introduced by Trump permanent but the financing for this is shaky and it can be assumed that the budget deficit will continue to swell, Commerzbank currency analyst Antje Praefcke says in a note. "I am curious to see when the market will realize that this is the next major structural burden on the dollar," she says. The DXY dollar index falls 0.3% to 99.6710. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0639 GMT - Sterling is little moved even after data showed U.K. retail sales rose by more than expected in April. Retail sales rose 1.2% month-on-month in April, helped by warm weather. Analysts in a WSJ survey expected a 0.3% rise. It follows a downwardly revised 0.1% increase in March. While the data are encouraging, higher-than-expected inflation in April could put the brakes on the possibility of another Bank of England rate cut for a few months, Ebury's Phil Monkhouse says in a note. Sterling rises 0.3% to $1.3453, little changed from levels before the data. The euro rises 0.1% to 0.8414 pounds, also near levels before the data. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0631 GMT - U.S. Treasury yields ease slightly, pulled lower by the long-end, but caution is warranted about the fragile recovery, says Commerzbank Research's Christoph Rieger in a note. "As U.S. Treasuries stage an impressive recovery we stay cautious while it is unclear whether Trump's 'big, beautiful bill' is teeing up U.S. Treasurys for a Liz Truss moment," the head of rates and credit research says. The U.S. House passed President Trump's tax-cut bill on Thursday. The two-year Treasury yield falls 1 basis point to 3.985%, the 10-year yield is down 2.4 basis points at 4.528% and the 30-year yield falls 2.6 basis points to 5.038%, according to LSEG data. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0615 GMT - Supply-demand imbalance in Japanese government bonds is at the core of weakness in long-dated JGBs, say Societe Generale's Stephen Spratt and Reo Sakida in a note. "Steady auction supply and accelerating quantitative tightening (QT) has increased bond supply as domestic investor demand has waned, and global term premiums have risen," the strategists say. This has been most evident in long-end JGBs, where Japan held the weakest 20-year JGB auction in almost 40 years and 30-year yields reached levels unseen since the series started, the strategists say. They expect JGB vulnerability to persist until either the Bank of Japan alters bond buying, the Ministry of Finance changes issuance composition, or demand rises. "None of which look likely near-term." (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0614 GMT - Eurozone government bond yield spreads over German Bunds look to be supported in the coming months, say Commerzbank Research's Christoph Rieger and Erik Liem in a note. Germany's fiscal paradigm shift underpins hopes about the eurozone's fiscal convergence, while U.S. President Trump and geopolitics act as catalysts to EU integration with hopes of more joint funding, the rates strategists say. Specifically for Italian-German yield spreads, relatively calm and stable Italian domestic politics is supportive, while rating risks appear to be muted until autumn, they say. The 10-year Italian BTP-German Bund yield spread broke below 100 bps this week, but for it to establish a new range below 100bps, "we don't see the prerequisites in place," the strategists say. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0552 GMT - Foreign investors added to their U.S. Treasury holdings in 1Q of 2025, although their preference was for the short end, say TD Securities' Jan Nevruzi​ and Gennadiy Goldberg​ in a note. "It is worth flagging​ that despite adding to Treasury holdings, foreign investors'​ increased their relative allocations to Treasury bill​s," the rates strategists say. This may have​ been a function of expected trade uncertainty, worries about term premium, a​ steeper yield curve, and a desire by official investors to keep more liquid assets​ on hand for potential currency intervention purposes​, they say. Both official and private foreign investors added to their bill holdings, and official investors accelerated their purchases more rapidly, the strategists say. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0543 GMT - New Zealand's central bank is likely to cut its official cash rate by 25 bps on May 28, and then cut just once more this year, say economists at HSBC Global Research. While a growth upswing is underway, timely data suggest the recovery isn't broad-based and consumers are still cautious, say Paul Bloxham and Jamie Culling in a note. Inflation is near the Reserve Bank of New Zealand's 1%-3% target band midpoint, but inflation expectations have edged higher, they say. Given the large amount of easing delivered, and uncertainty about global developments and how long this will take to feed through to the economy, the economists expect RBNZ to adopt a more cautious approach to the outlook. The RBNZ cut its official cash rate to 3.50% from 3.75% in April. (monica.gupta@wsj.com)

0539 GMT - German Bunds in particular are expected to benefit from a rotation away from U.S. Treasurys, potentially anchoring 10-year Bund yields around 2.50% in the coming months, says SEB Research's Jussi Hiljanen in a note. "We expect increasing interest in Bunds as an alternative to U.S. Treasuries to balance the upside pressure on Bund yields resulting from eurozone government bond supply and the Eurosystem balance sheet reduction," the chief rates strategist says. Interest-rate cuts by the European Central Bank should also help prevent Bund yields from rising significantly, he says. "We think the German 10-year yield will fluctuate mostly near 2.50% in the coming months," he says. The 10-year Bund yield closed at 2.63% on Thursday, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0534 GMT - Long-dated U.S. Treasury yields are prone to some further rise, driven partly by fading confidence in U.S. policy, says SEB Research's Jussi Hiljanen in a note. "An erosion of trust in U.S. policy, unattractive valuations when accounting for FX hedging costs, and rotation into European bonds suggest structural upside pressure on long U.S. yields," the chief rates strategist says. SEB Research forecasts a modest increase in long yields, but fiscal policy is a risk that could trigger a significant repricing of Treasurys, he says. The 10-year Treasury yield falls 1.5 basis points to 4.537%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0418 GMT - The Malaysian ringgit is poised to appreciate further as global investors rotate out of U.S. assets, despite lingering domestic headwinds from tariff-related uncertainties, Kenanga IB economists say in a note. They now forecast the ringgit to end 2025 at 4.08 against the greenback, stronger than their earlier estimate of 4.45. The revision reflects a shift in capital towards more stable emerging market assets, amid mounting U.S. fiscal concerns following Trump's push for tax cuts and a sovereign rating downgrade, they say. Technically, the pair may remain range-bound around 4.27, with external developments continuing to serve as the primary catalyst, they add. Kenanga expects USD/MYR to face resistance at 4.28 and find support at 4.24 in the short term. USD/MYR is 0.5% lower at 4.2547. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Dow Jones Top Markets Headlines at 3 AM ET: U.S. Stocks Settle Mixed | House ...
data.description:
U.S. Stocks Settle Mixed

Major stock indexes were mixed, while bitcoin prices held near new record highs. The Nasdaq Composite rose, while the S&P 500 edged lower. The Dow was flat.

----

House Passes Trump Tax Bill After Last-Minute Changes

The sprawling tax-and-spending bill passed after GOP leaders made a series of last-minute changes that united their warring wings.

----

Americans Aren't Buying Trump's Economic Agenda. The Numbers Prove It.

President Trump insists the economy is booming. Americans aren't so sure.

----

Rate Cuts May Not Happen as Quickly-or Even at All This Year, Fed's Collins Says

----

It's Time to Worry About Japan and the Yen Carry Trade Again

Moderate moves to tighten monetary policy in Japan are disrupting markets on both sides of the Pacific Ocean.

----

Trump's Memecoin Dinner Draws Crypto Tycoons, a Basketball Star and Protests

The cryptocurrency industry's lovefest with MAGA reached a pinnacle Thursday when President Trump hosted the top buyers of his memecoin at his golf club outside Washington.

----

Global Finance Officials Paper Over Trade Tensions At G-7 Summit

Finance and central bank chiefs from the Group of Seven nations offered few details about their economic summit. U.S. officials have discussed whether allies would work toward a unified tariff policy on China to pressure Beijing.

----

Chinese Drugmaker's Shares Jump in Strong Hong Kong Debut

Shares of Jiangsu Hengrui Pharmaceuticals soared Friday morning, as more companies seek to tap Hong Kong's capital markets.

----

Fed Overnight Lending Facility to Grow in Importance, New York Fed Official Says

As the central bank's balance sheet shrinks, the standing repo facility will likely take on greater importance in capping borrowing costs, Roberto Perli says.

----

Central U.S. Manufacturing Dips as Price Pressures Cool-Kansas City Fed

The manufacturing index edged up slightly in May to minus 3-still indicating a contraction-from minus 4 in April.

----

U.S. Economic Growth Slowed in Turbulent April, Chicago Fed Index Shows

Economic growth in the U.S. slowed in April, according to a monthly index Thursday, a likely byproduct of increased market uncertainty from the Trump administration's trade policy launched at the start of the month.

----

Trump's War Against Higher Education Hits the Sector's Bonds

The Trump administration's moves against prominent colleges and universities are spreading concerns that a wide swath of higher-education institutions could see their borrowing costs rise, while some investors could end up with a win.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Company Headlines at 3 AM ET: Big Banks Explore Venturing Into Crypto World Together With Joint Stablecoin | Federal ...
data.description:
Big Banks Explore Venturing Into Crypto World Together With Joint Stablecoin

The discussions involve payments companies co-owned by JPMorgan Chase, other large banks.

----

Federal Trade Commission Drops Pricing Lawsuit Against PepsiCo

The lawsuit alleged the beverage company provided promotional pricing deals to a single big-box customer but didn't give the same incentives to other retailers.

----

Oaktree in Takeover Talks With Superior Industries

The top lender, advised by law firm Paul Weiss, is in discussions to take over the aluminum wheel maker struggling with auto parts tariffs.

----

Chinese Drugmaker's Shares Jump in Strong Hong Kong Debut

Shares of Jiangsu Hengrui Pharmaceuticals soared Friday morning, as more companies seek to tap Hong Kong's capital markets.

----

Longer-term tariff predictions have proven too tough for these footwear and clothing companies. It's derailing their stocks.

Executives at Ross Stores and Deckers say they will not provide full-year guidance

----

Hinge Health Goes Public, Delivering Much-Needed Win for Digital-Health Startups

The NYSE debut is the first major IPO for a venture-backed digital-health company since the pandemic-era boom.

----

Fortescue Energy CEO to Resign in Executive Overhaul

Mark Hutchinson will step down as Dino Otranto, who leads the miner's metals business, assumes an expanded role.

----

Rooftop Solar Takes Gut Punch in House Tax Bill

The bill sunsets renewable energy credits, as expected, but includes more stringent provisions and rollback dates that were seen as especially bleak for rooftop solar.

----

Goodyear Tire to Sell Majority of Chemical Business for $650 Million

Gemspring Capital Management will acquire chemical facilities in Houston and Beaumont, Texas, as well as a related research office in Akron, Ohio.

----

Intuit Seeing Stable Environment for Consumers, Businesses While Tariffs Play Out

Stronger-than-expected results in the latest quarter prompted the financial technology platform to raise its outlook for the year.

----

Workday Logs Higher Revenue, Backs Outlook; Initiates Buyback Program

The human-resources software company logged higher first-quarter revenue and reaffirmed its full-year outlook as sales in its key subscription segment continue to rise.

----

Autodesk Boosts Full-Year Guidance After Increase in Quarterly Sales

The software company now expects full-year revenue between $6.925 billion and $6.995 billion, up $30 million on either end of the range. Shares rose in after-hours trading.

data.title: All samples analyzed from the 30 mg dose group in the NEX-22 Phase I study
data.description:
Nanexa announced today that all pharmacokinetic (PK) samples from the final dose group, 30 mg of the Phase I study for NEX-22, now have been analyzed. The results show increased exposure in line with the dose escalation and further demonstrate a controlled and prolonged release of liraglutide, which supports a one-month depot of liraglutide.

“The full results of the NEX-22 study will be presented at scientific congresses, with participation at the ADA (American Diabetes Association) in June being one of the highlights,” says David Westberg, CEO of Nanexa. “The results also strengthen our position in our discussions with potential partners for the NEX-22 project.”

data.title: China Stocks Fall for Second Session
data.description:
The Shanghai Composite fell 0.94% to close at 3,348 while the Shenzhen Component dropped 0.85% to 10,132 on Friday, with mainland stocks sliding for the second straight session as selling pressure persisted despite positive developments on the Sino-US trade front.

China’s Foreign Ministry said Beijing and Washington had agreed to maintain open lines of communication following a diplomatic call between Vice Foreign Minister Ma Zhaoxu and US Deputy Secretary of State Kurt Campbell.

While the move eased some geopolitical concerns, it failed to lift market sentiment meaningfully.

Meanwhile, the People’s Bank of China injected 500 billion yuan into the financial system via its one-year medium-term lending facility, reinforcing its commitment to supporting credit growth amid ongoing economic fragility.

Notable drags on the indexes included losses in major names such as Contemporary Amperex (-1.2%), East Money Information (-2.1%), and Kunlun Tech (-2.5%).

data.title: Aldoro Resources Says Drilling at Namibia Project Confirms Extension to Polymetallic Discovery
data.description:
Aldoro Resources said assay results from diamond drilling confirm a significant south-west extension to the polymetallic discovery at the Kameelburg project in Namibia, comprising rare earth elements and molybdenum, according to a Friday ASX filing.

A "significant" intercept encountered during the drilling came in at 293 meters at 3.67% grade of total rare earth oxide-equivalent from 3 meters, comprising 2.1% grade of total rare earth oxides, 0.21% grade of niobium oxide, and 448 parts-per-million of molybdenum.

It is expecting to receive the remaining assays from the project in the coming weeks, and it plans to use them to compile a maiden mineral resource estimate for the project.

Its shares jumped 10% on market close on Friday.

data.title: Saurashtra Cement Records Growth in Fiscal Q4 Consolidated Net Profit
data.description:
Saurashtra Cement witnessed a rise in its consolidated net profit to 329.1 million Indian rupees in the fiscal fourth quarter ended March 31, against 310.8 million rupees a year ago.

Earnings per share came in at 2.96 rupees compared with a year-ago figure of 2.80 rupees, according to a Thursday filing to the Indian stock exchanges by the cement manufacturer.

Revenue from operations in fiscal Q4, however, fell to 4.78 billion rupees from 4.97 billion rupees a year earlier.

The company's shares were up 3% in recent trade.

data.title: Hiscox share price rises 7% as investors welcome updated guidance
data.description:
(The Insurer) - Hiscox's share price closed up more than 7% on Thursday after the London-listed insurer announced a series of new targets at its capital markets day, including plans to achieve double-digit retail premium growth by 2028.

With retail set to represent an increasing proportion of the business over time, Hiscox's board has guided towards delivering a mid-teens operating return on equity through the cycle.

The new guidance also pointed towards a 20% step up in Hiscox's final dividend per share for 2025, which follows a 15% increase in 2024.

Hiscox said its interim dividend policy will be to pay one-third of its prior year total ordinary dividend per share, providing more clarity on mid-year capital returns.

In addition, Hiscox said it will accelerate its change agenda to deliver cost savings of $200 million from 2028 onwards, compared with a 2024 baseline.

Most of this will be recognised in its retail business, Hiscox said.

KBW analyst Darius Satkauskas said the announcements highlighted that the group was now in a stronger position to profitably grow its retail business, at a time when rates for big-ticket lines are starting to soften.

data.title: Euro area benchmark Bund yields set for fifth straight weekly rise
data.description:
Euro area benchmark Bund yields dipped on Friday but were on track for their fifth straight weekly rise, tracking moves in U.S. Treasuries as fiscal concerns dominated market sentiment.

The Republican-controlled U.S. House of Representatives on Thursday passed a sweeping tax and spending bill that could saddle the country with trillions of dollars more in debt.

Germany's 10-year yield (DE10YT=RR) was down 2.5 basis points (bps) at 2.62%, but still on track for a weekly rise of 3 bps, while the 30-year yields (DE30YT=RR) were about to end the week 8 bps higher.

U.S. Treasury yields edged down in early London trade – with the 10-year falling 3 bps to 4.52% — after dropping on Thursday as the recent selloff drew some buyers at more attractive levels. The 10-year and 30-year (US30YT=RR) yields were set for their biggest weekly rise since early April, when a U.S. Treasury selloff drove 10-year yields up 50 bps.

Money markets priced in the European Central Bank deposit facility rate to be at 1.72% by year-end (EURESTECBM5X6=ICAP).

They also indicated more than a 95% chance of a rate cut in June and an about 20% chance of a second easing move in July. The depo rate is currently at 2.25%.

German 2-year yields (DE2YT=RR), more sensitive to ECB policy rates, were down 1.5 bps at 1.81%.

Italy's 10-year yield fell 2.5 bps to 3.64% . The spread between Italian and German yields – a market gauge of the risk premium investors demand to hold Italian debt – was at 99.5 bps (DE10IT10=RR).

data.title: Agility Public Warehousing Halts Trading on Dubai Bourse
data.description:
Agility Public Warehousing (DFM:AGLTY) paused trading on the Dubai Financial Market on Friday, according to a same-day release.

The suspension was due to the supply chain services company's inability to publish the results of its general assembly.

data.title: ChiNext Price Index Ends 1.2% Lower at 2021.50
India's Indoco Remedies gains after US FDA approval for uric acid regulating drug
data.description:
** India's Indoco Remedies up 2% at 247.56 rupees

** Co gets approval from U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for Allopurinol tablets, used to prevent or lower uric acid levels in the blood

** Approval to be positive for growth in the U.S. market, says co

** Including session's gains, stock trims 12-month decline to 20.6%

data.title: KOSPI Closes Slightly Lower
data.description:
The benchmark KOSPI edged down 0.1% to close at 2,592 on Friday, erasing earlier gains, and posted a 1.3% weekly loss, its first decline in six weeks, as market caution lingered over US President Trump’s tax cut bill.

Recently passed by the House of Representatives, investors continued to assess its implications, projected to expand the US debt burden by $3.8 trillion over the next decade.

In response, South Korea’s acting Finance Minister said Friday that authorities will closely monitor domestic markets amid increased bond market volatility in the US and Japan.

Meanwhile, fresh data showing producer inflation slowed has reinforced bets that the Bank of Korea may implement a 25 bps rate cut in its upcoming policy meeting.

Among index heavyweights, Samsung Electronics (-0.9%), Samsung Biologics (-5.9%), LG Energy Solutions (-2.4%), and Hyundai Motor (-1.4%) led the losses.

Conversely, top gainers include chip giant SK Hynix (1.6%) and financial stocks, such as KB Financial (2.4%).

data.title: Flutter Entertainment Prices Notes due 2031
data.description:
Flutter Entertainment's Flutter Treasury DAC unit priced its offering of senior secured notes due 2031, according to a Friday release.

The online sports betting company priced $1 billion of 5.875% notes, 550 million euros of 4% notes, and 450 million pounds sterling of 6.125% notes.

The proceeds are expected to be used to repay a bridge facility that helped fund the acquisition of Snaitech and for general corporate use.
